summary: Drew DeVault describes an April Fool's joke project that didn't quite make it: 'git snail-mail,' a tool that prints git commits on paper and mails them to maintainers. He actually wrote most of the code, prototyping it in his then-secret programming language (Hare), using TeX templates to format patches as printable PDFs. The project was cancelled because he didn't want to reveal the secret language and couldn't bring himself to rewrite it in Perl. He shares the technical details of how it works and links to other joke projects that did ship.
key_points: The tool generates TeX-formatted patches and pipes them through pdflatex and lp(1) to print physical copies of git commits
He prototyped it in his secret language (Hare, before its public announcement) and couldn't bring himself to rewrite it in Perl for upstream inclusion
He improved on git send-email's confusing commit selection by using standard git revision ranges instead
The post is a rare lighthearted, self-deprecating entry that still manages to showcase real code and technical chops
He links to several completed joke projects including a git implementation in POSIX shell and an IRC bot in brainfuck
money_quotes: You've heard of git-over-email thanks to git send-email — now you can enjoy git snail-mail: a new tool making it easier than ever to print out git commits on paper and mail them to your maintainers.
Be sure to help the maintainers review your work by including a return envelope and a stamp!
For maintainers, code review has never been easier — just get out your red marker and write your feedback directly on the patch!
I could not really rouse the enthusiasm for implementing this idea in Perl.
After prototyping in $secretlang I could not bring myself to rewrite it in Perl, and the joke fell flat.
Not every idea pans out, but they're still worth trying, anyway.
key_insight: Even joke projects can be worth pursuing for the craft and fun of building them, and sometimes the best April Fool's prank is the one that accidentally becomes a real prototype.
